item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you both, I appreciate the evaluations! It feels nice to know that others feel that reading and commenting on what I've made is worth their time and effort. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just saying: it adds to it for me. Aren't you interested in seeing what, exactly, is down there? Wouldn't it be even more interesting if it, whatever it was, basically just like a human? Just my opinion as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does anyone else think this article might be better if there was no entity? It's a bit cliche and takes away some of the mystery, and seems out of place to me. Just my opinion, and otherwise it's great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation scientists realized the language -1 was using through trial and error. This may seem too large a task for the scientists to have completed in a timely manner, but with the recent irl development of quantum computers, which happen to be excellent at this kind of thing, let's assume that the Foundation got their hands on such technology well before we did. The rest is explained in the article:

SCP-2929-1 originally broadcast a loop of eight parabolic figures in a line, with each figure extending and depressing at different intervals. On-location researchers realized this could be interpreted as representing the numbers 0 to 128 in binary, with "peaks" representing "1s" and "troughs" representing "0s," followed by a seemingly arbitrary series of numbers.

On-location cryptographers and linguists found that by using a simple substitution code with a previously unheard of dialect bearing similarities to ancient Khoisan languages, the second series of binary numbers could be interpreted as a greeting followed by a phrase akin to "SENDWAVES."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
on-site technicians directed a focused gamma ray wave transmission of a greeting in modern Sandawe by binary in the direction of SCP-2929-1

This does not make sense. To convert a language to binary, there needs to be an agreed upon standard. Ascii, unicode, etc. Are examples of standards. While a 001101 to us might be an A to us, to him it might be a Q. How did they ensure he would understand it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It hurts, but it works. Sometimes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah yes. This just serves to prove that sometimes, the feedback system works exactly as intended. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for appreciating the changes! You might not know this, but I made 100% of those changes from my phone, as I do not have access to my computer. It was about as difficult as you could expect, and the most troubling factor was probably that copy-pasting is extremely difficult on iOS for whatever reason. My next challenge is uploading a photo, which is already underway! The folks down at the Help Chat were very helpful and I plan to give them shout outs as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am very much in favour of the new edits, and have switched my vote to a +1. I particularly like the fact that, despite removing the "dark ones", you have added a note of ambiguity at the end - it serves three purposes: undermining slightly the general feel-good factor of the piece, it reflects your stance in the discussion here, and it lampshades some of the pieces of the description that don't quite fit (such as use of binary). That is a deft trick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 谢谢！

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
YESH!

sirpudding approval count: 0 1

Thanks! I am trying to figure out how to attach an image, and I am very slowly making progress. If I end up needing my computer, it might take until after Sunday to get it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much improved with revision.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much improved and vote reversed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will make the change shortly.

e: Finished.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think that you are describing a real world technology here, although I could be wrong.

It isn't, at least not yet. It's the sort of thing that navies would play with (probably before other military branches), since having sufficient power and space to mess with prototype technology isn't usually a problem on ships. And I'm fairly sure most of the work they're doing (in as much as they publicly acknowledge such things) is built around countering radar rather than visual light. Cloaking devices as we think of them in fiction simply do not exist, and the closest analogues that do are sufficiently imperfect that you'll notice the hidden object if it is against a solid, not-textured background (like a clear sky). They also require obscene amounts of power, which would make an application of the sort described in the containment procedures highly impractical (and expensive).

Further, they would not prevent there from being a shadow from the camouflaging structure itself, which at sunrise and sunset could easily be kilometers long. Given the terrain in Katavi National Park, the Foundation has built a 200 meter tall sundial that any satellite overpass or airplane flying at standard cruising altitude for several hundred kilometers around would have no trouble seeing. (Which is another point - a 200 meter tall structure presents an aviation hazard; most places in the United States require appropriate warning lights on structures taller than 200 feet, and the International Civil Aviation Organization has global standards which most countries follow.) It's just simply not possible to make something that tall which can't be noticed by someone with an appropriate vantage point. A better solution would be to come up with some plausible structure of appropriate height (a watchtower for wildfire prevention, a radio tower, etc., though 200 meters is a bit tall for most things) and explain it away that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Projection camouflage is totally dependent on knowing the exact position of the observer and isn't holographic. It certainly works better the smaller the thing you are hiding is and probably doesn't scale well to a 200 m high giant LCD fence. I don't think that you are describing a real world technology here, although I could be wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am changing the log, but it might be a bit.

e: I have changed the log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am changing the log, but it might be a bit.

e: I have changed the log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OK, that is fine. I would like to again thank you for the effort you have put in to help me through the revision process. I really appreciate it.

especially if you choose to ignore the advice of multiple established authors giving you the same advice

I know that it seems like I am completely ignoring the advice I have received since I have published this, but I have simply been too busy in the past couple of days to sit down and get cracking. You might not believe me, but it is the truth. I would like to apologize.

the stated origin is cliche.

Before I say anything, I would like to make it clear that my tone is incorrect. I'm not trying to be hostile, but it might sound that way. *Ahem*
Is it? Is the origin of -1 common and overused in some way? I honestly cannot recall ever reading of an ancient individual transferring his mind into a rock in the lower mantle to escape an apocalypse. Care to provide an example where this concept has been used?

So. What I think I might do is change the log completely. Honestly, this will probably sever any connection to 1050. I would take out the footnote on the language pretty easily as well. Is this OK with you? I am not going to like it, and it is not happening any time soon due to reasons beyond my control.

Anyway, it will likely be a few days until any major edits can be done, as I will not have access to my computer for nine days or so. I could try using my iPad, but it is much clunkier and will not be as effective as my computer. Sorry.

e: Finished.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Echoing the "great setup, disappointing payoff" comments.

Neutral voting because I want to see this improved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would be honored, Hornby, if you would write the doctors note and send it to me over my vacation so the best results are received. sirpudding could give it a shot too if he wants. If both of you decline (which you may, of course), then this will not happen for over a week. Actually, anyone could try. just send it to me for review and I will see what I can do.

This doesn't work that way. It's your article, not mine.

I've offered you advice and constructive criticism; I've even suggested to you a different write-up of the transcript in the log, predicated on your desire to keep the warning-of-doom aspect in this SCP (an aspect I have and continue to advocate that you either tone down or remove outright). You've taken some of that advice, and declined to take other parts, which is fine because it's your article, not mine.1 If I were (re)writing this, I'd go a different direction entirely. I don't like seeing writers fail, have a hard-learned understanding of how critical and tough-to-please this community can be, and know how discouraging it can be when blunt-to-a-fault criticism gets handed down; because of this, and because you reached out to me, I've tried to offer the best advice I can.

To put none too fine a point on it, I don't much like the general "mind of someone hiding from civilization-ending-abominations" origin/explanation as you use it in this article. It's cliche. It's cliche whether or not we take 2929-1 at its word (another point I'll get to in a second). Something very similar has also been done before elsewhere on the site2. I know from your PMs that you wanted to write something related to 1050, and I'm flattered that you liked that article, but I'm afraid I'm not that fond of how you've chosen to go about doing it. None of this means you can't write your article your way, but nothing says your article is going to be well-received or highly-rated (especially if you choose to ignore the advice of multiple established authors giving you the same advice).

On the subject of whether or not to take -1 at its word, there needs to be something about the object itself to give the Foundation and the readers pause. Remember, SCP-2929's claims are hardly the most absurd thing the Foundation would have encountered. A simple "doctor's note" isn't going to cut it for most people, myself included, since it doesn't change the underlying facts surrounding the object. And even if we have good reason to believe that -1 isn't telling the truth, willfully or otherwise, it does not change the fact that the stated origin is cliche.

So you can leave the article as is, or add a "doctor's note" to suggest we shouldn't automatically believe what/whoever is in the rock, or add/change details about the object itself to suggest we shouldn't automatically believe it, or you can scrap the entire Dark Ones premise in the dialogue (replacing it with something else or not). That's all up to you. I've given you my thoughts, but I'm afraid I'm not in the mood to do your creative work for you.

Footnotes
1. I'll be honest, this is much improved over the first draft you showed me, for what that's worth.
2. And I linked you to that article while you were initially soliciting feedback on your draft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes. I agree. Amen. I already talked to sirpudding about appending a doctor's note to put the legitimacy of -1's claims into question. Sadly, I am leaving for vacation today and will not have access to my computer to write for more than a week. However, I can still receive and send (to a limited degree) PMs from my phone. I would be honored, Hornby, if you would write the doctors note and send it to me over my vacation so the best results are received. sirpudding could give it a shot too if he wants. If both of you decline (which you may, of course), then this will not happen for over a week. Actually, anyone could try. just send it to me for review and I will see what I can do. Thanks, and no pressure.

Additionally:

apparently generated coherently by an intelligence other than a regular old human being

Well, not necessarily. Maybe it is telling the truth. But maybe not.

even to the point of questioning whether or not the giant rock-that-writes is in fact anything other than pure random chance which just so happens to create symbols that correspond with an intelligible dialogue on the part of the researchers.

That was a hyperbole. I didn't actually mean that, I just wanted to convey the uncertainty of the situation.

suggests that both should be given more serious consideration by researchers than they would be lacking that sort of corroboration.

A very good point. (Disclaimer: I am not disputing this) however, do keep in mind the reasons not to trust 2929-1 (or even 1050). For example, is it more likely that 2929 and 2929-1 were created by very weirdly advanced Mesolithic Tanzanians, or that, say, a more plausibly1 advanced entity or entities created 2929 and 2929-1 for unknown purposes? This is not a rhetorical question per se, but you (Hornby) don't need to answer it if you don't want to. It is just something to think about. In other words, if the Foundation researchers used Occam's Razor to speculate about the origins of 2929-1, would they have actually reached the conclusions they did in the final paragraph? I'm not implying an answer to that question, fyi.

the article for 1050 both includes details that indicate that all is not as it seems

Could you please provide an example?

Thank you, Hornby.

Footnotes
1. As in, an entity or entities for which the ability to create 2929 and 2929-1 are to be expected or more likely/reasonable to suspect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The problem, ooblex, is that if you want the reader to not take 2929-1 at its word, then you need some indication in the article to suggest that they shouldn't.

To put it bluntly, you're asking us to be credulous towards the fact that there is a globe-spanning, top secret organization which can make convincing-to-the-point-of-being-an-invisibility-cloak holograms that are hundreds of meters tall, and that there is a kilometers-long piece of obsidian that constantly alters its surface to create patterns clear enough to be interpreted as language (apparently generated coherently by an intelligence other than a regular old human being), and then you're asking us to be incredulous as to what this giant rock-that-writes is saying - even to the point of questioning whether or not the giant rock-that-writes is in fact anything other than pure random chance which just so happens to create symbols that correspond with an intelligible dialogue on the part of the researchers. So, would you like us to suspend disbelief, or not? The reader is likely to follow both Occam's Razor (the simplest explanation is best in the absence of evidence to the contrary) and Law of Conservation of Detail (writers rarely use up valuable space to mention irrelevant details, and the shorter the work of fiction, the less likely any particular detail is to be a product of random chance and/or not plot relevant).

It is true that we (both the Doylist readers and Watsonian members of the Foundation) have little corroborating evidence to suggest we ought to take 2929-1 at its word. Ten-fifty exists, and the fact that you've made a cross-link (about which I'm not complaining, mind you) gives some corroborating evidence. The fact that both give issue a warning, the shared previously known script, and that there is not an apparent shared origin (i.e., not both made by the same GoI) suggests that both should be given more serious consideration by researchers than they would be lacking that sort of corroboration. The difference is that the article for 1050 both includes details that indicate that all is not as it seems and includes clear delineations between what the Foundation knows (fact) vs thinks (educated guesses). While the article represents a prevailing viewpoint, others which are mutually exclusive exist which cannot be definitively debunked. All of your comments about how we should take 2929-1 with a grain of salt are in the meta-text, not the text itself. Votes reflect the article as a stand-alone piece of fiction, not (at least not necessarily) the commentary the author provides in the discussion thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I desperately wish that whatever may or may not be in the rock had something more interesting to say, because the rest of this is excellent and fun to read. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Regardless of whether I am required to imagine your skip in a vacuum or not, it still leads me to to wonder why this technology isn't in widespread use and how it actually works. It is also lazy writing. You couldn't be bothered to come up with something plausible, so instead you are putting the stress on my belief suspenders. Well, they snapped.

…It actually is based on real-world technology. "Projection camouflage" refers to a real-world technology sometimes referred to as "Retro-Reflective Projection Technology." You might recognize a prototype example in the famous "invisibility cloak" device seen in this image. If you want to learn more about it, I suggest reading about it in the Wikipedia article on active camouflage.

I realize I said "I made [it] up," that was a mistake. I took real technology and imagined it on a much more massive scale.

I responded to the second part of your comment in a PM.

Anyway, no hard feelings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry to let you down, Hornby.

Please read my reply to Baby Boo. I get what you mean with Fridge Horror, but I would like to clarify that I did not and do not intend to make 2929 "scary." I'm pretty sure that is not what you were implying, but just in case. I think I might have been too subtle (although I thought the last paragraph would have at least helped get the message across), as most of the comments so far seem to indicate. This is why I appended my quote in the first comment.

Thanks for all the help and advice, by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ This One Is a Doozy
- I Warned You

The basic concept of a big-rock-that-speaks is an intriguing start, rife with possibilities, but it's a letdown that it just turns out to be some guy hiding from a bog-standard All-Destroyer-Who-Must-Not-Be-Named (literally, I slumped and groaned aloud on reaching "TO HIDE FROM THE DARK ONES").

Please see what I wrote in response to the previous two comments:

"I know it is cliche, but only if you actually believe what -1 "tells" you. It could have no idea what it is talking about. It could be confused. It could be desperate for attention after who-knows-how long alone and cannot maintain its composure for long until it ends up sulking and moping (I particularly like this one). It could be lying. The Foundation might be misinterpreting the patterns completely; perhaps -1 is not sentient at all, and the patterns on 2929 are simply random (although extremely improbable) products of a probabilistic universe. The reader and the Foundation simply do not know." -ooblex, 2015

A big stone pillar that undulates and sometimes spikes 250 meters high has escaped notice by the mundane world throughout all of human history?

This is a very valid point, one I had come across when I had first started my draft. I considered tying in real-world local mythology1, but I thought it might unnecessarily complicate things. I might, just might, put it in some time after I get back from my upcoming vacation.

A possibly Paleolithic individual, whose ancient precursor-Khoisan language almost certainly lacked a written form, communicates by transliterating its language in a "simple substitution" using binary code? A substitution of what — is it producing hexadecimal code for a Latin-alphabet phonetic spelling, or… what? It would be easier to believe if it just produced visible letters on its top surface.

OK, OK. I know there is no "Khoisan alphabet," or even a written form for Khoisan languages, excluding modern phonetic attempts. I chose to leave this plot hole where is because, to be frank, a majority of readers probably haven't even heard of Khoisan languages (that is not an insult to anyone), much less know that they do not have writing systems. I felt it would be unnecessary to maneuver around the discrepancy and that it would distract me from more important issues, of which there were many. I applaud you for being the first person to realize this issue and dub thee the Khoisan Knight. Congrats. :P Also, please keep in mind the culture, if any, that -1 possibly belonged to was bewilderingly and anachronistically advanced. That is part of what is so anomalous and mysterious about its origins.

This message in binary is "similar" to an unknown script? In what way?

I dunno. I don't speak Khoisan. I assumed I would be granted some hand-waving for that.

Given the message "SENDWAVES", was the researchers' first thought to use gamma rays? Did they try radio first, or even just a big set of speakers?

Good point. But due to how deep underground -1 actually is, gamma rays are really the only way to effectively transmit something to it (or at least the only one I could think of). This is the same reason why GRI was necessary.

Plan A is "a big tarp" and Plan B is "a 300m-high holographic projection"? What is a camouflage projection? Can it only be seen from directly above? What are their plans to hide this object from seismologists around the world?

It is more like Plan A is "a 200m-high holographic projection camouflage structure" with "a big suspended tarp," Plan A was scrapped for reasons detailed in the article (the tarp was shredded and it was out-of-date anyway, etc.), and Plan B is "a 300m-high holographic projection camouflage structure" and "make sure no one takes a picture of it from a satellite."
"Projection camouflage" refers to a real-world technology sometimes referred to as "Retro-Reflective Projection Technology." You might recognize a prototype example in the famous "invisibility cloak" device seen in this image. If you want to learn more about it, I suggest reading about it in the Wikipedia article on active camouflage. It is pretty cool.
Basically, the structure is like a fence surrounding the perimeter for the exposed end of 2929, so it would not stop views from above.
The tremors associated with 2929 are too small to be considered worthy of research to any competent seismologist. Even if the seismologist is not competent, would a seismologist really go to the site of a small tremor to somehow investigate? I highly doubt it.

What is noteworthy about the numbers mentioned in footnotes 2 and 3?

I wanted to further illustrate how perfectly crafted (if that is the appropriate word) 2929 is.

I was right there with you even despite some of the peculiar bits

What peculiar bits in particular?

Find some other identity and origin for the subterranean mass of post-perovskite and I think you can reform it into a winner.

The problem is, if I were to change the nature and origin of the subterranean mass of post-perovskite, how in the world would the Foundation be able to figure that out? Even in the article as it is, the Foundation doesn't really know what its nature is or its origins, the Foundation not knowing the latter of which I thought I made pretty clear with my final paragraph. The only things the Foundation has to go on are the patterns on the exposed surface of 2929, the GRI readings, and -1's vague and possibly false claims. The patterns tell them what -1 is "saying," the GRI readings tell them the shape, size, and composition of 2929 and -1, and -1's claims cannot be taken as truth for certain. So the Foundation can only speculate its nature and origins, and nothing more.


I hope this helps you to see the article from a different perspective. Thanks for the input.

e: I made a huge edit.

Footnotes
1. Michael Atreus also happened to come across this later on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I cannot go back in time and persuade dozens of authors to use a tool for containment that I made up, nor would I want to. There is no central canon for skips, and I intend to make use of that fact. My skip could exist in an entirely separate universe from any other skip (other than 1050, of course). Also, are you sure it is implausible that the foundation has this useful ultra-tech? We are talking about the same Foundation, right? Sorry for the sarcasm.

That's not the point. Special Containment Procedures are interesting when they show ingenuity in solving occult problems with mundane means, IMO. "Gee willikers, Doctor Blackbox! Why don't we just shoot it with the normalization ray, call it a day, and go out for chocolate malts?" is not interesting. Regardless of whether I am required to imagine your skip in a vacuum or not, it still leads me to to wonder why this technology isn't in widespread use and how it actually works. It is also lazy writing. You couldn't be bothered to come up with something plausible, so instead you are putting the stress on my belief suspenders. Well, they snapped.

I apologize for the bluntness of the above, I tried to rephrase it in a less harsh way, but I failed to
do so and retain the meaning. I am sorry.

Things are not always as they seem. Are you really going to take -1's claims as truth?

If there's a hint that the entity is unreliable, it is so subtle that I missed it. Even if it is unreliable the claim itself is still cliche and nothing textual in your piece (as opposed to word of God) subverts it (that I am able to see).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't find it plausible that the Foundation has this useful ultra-tech, keeps it secret, and only uses it here (it's even "standard issue" technobabble here, if it is standard issue why isn't it used everywhere it would make sense?).

I cannot go back in time and persuade dozens of authors to use a tool for containment that I made up, nor would I want to. There is no central canon for skips, and I intend to make use of that fact. My skip could exist in an entirely separate universe from any other skip (other than 1050, of course). Also, are you sure it is implausible that the foundation has this useful ultra-tech? We are talking about the same Foundation, right? Sorry for the sarcasm.

The final payoff of the communication with the entity is such a cliche letdown.

Things are not always as they seem. Are you really going to take -1's claims as truth? Please see my response to the comment before this one, particularly:

+ This
- Collapse

I know it is cliche, but only if you actually believe what -1 "tells" you. It could have no idea what it is talking about. It could be confused. It could be desperate for attention after who-knows-how long alone and cannot maintain its composure for long until it ends up sulking and moping (I particularly like this one). It could be lying. The Foundation might be misinterpreting the patterns completely; perhaps -1 is not sentient at all, and the patterns on 2929 are simply random (although extremely improbable) products of a probabilistic universe. The reader and the Foundation simply do not know.

I appreciate your input and I hope you reconsider your downvote. If you do not, I understand. No biggy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just to clarify:

I too was a bit disappointed that it turned out to be a man in the rock

Why is that? Also, see the footnote on this comment.

particularly the "they're coming! You can't run!" sort of dialogue in the interview.

I know it is cliche, but only if you actually believe what -1 "tells" you. It could have no idea what it is talking about. It could be confused. It could be desperate for attention after who-knows-how long alone and cannot maintain its composure for long until it ends up sulking and moping (I particularly like this one). It could be lying. The Foundation might be misinterpreting the patterns completely; perhaps -1 is not sentient at all, and the patterns on 2929 are simply random (although extremely improbable) products of a probabilistic universe. The reader and the Foundation simply do not know.

Not sure why he would go to all the bother

Go to all the bother to do what?

or even if he could.

The culture, if any, that the man1 belonged to was bewilderingly and anachronistically advanced. That is part of what is so anomalous and mysterious about its origins.

Surely just hiding in a big rock would do it?

…Do what?

btw, thanks for the input.

e: I replaced the log and changed a lot.

Footnotes
1. It is not actually certain that "he" was a man at all; "he" just claimed to be one a long time ago. -1 might just be a confused or intentionally deceptive sapient chunk of post-perovskite. Or maybe it was some non-human sapient entity (say, an alien) that identifies as a "man," since aliens (entities) would probably not call each other aliens (or entities), but "men," "women," "people," or some other identifier. In other words, from an alien's or other sapient entity's point of view, we are the aliens/entities and they are "people" or "men." I'm not saying -1 actually is a man, a confused rock, an alien, or a member of an ancient race of sapient creatures, but these are all possibilities.

item.title: Re: Special Thanks
item.content:encoded: Pleasure & privilege as ever; liked in in draft, like it polished up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Though I've stated this in PMs, for the record, I basically agree with this assessment, both in the broad sense and in the specifics. (I am also declining to vote for the time being.)

There are interesting things that can be done with the thinking-talking-rock idea - and it most certainly is an interesting idea, at least in theory - but I personally think that the civilization-ending-eldritch-abominations-of-doom is one of the weaker ones you could run with, especially the more explicitly you choose to do so. (I sometimes suspect that 1050 is rated as high as much in spite of that aspect as because of it.)

Remember the old adage about showing rather than telling. If you tell me "there's a ghost in the room," I probably won't believe you and almost certainly won't be scared. If you show me what looks like a ghost in the room, I'll be more inclined to believe you, but it'd have to be pretty convincing to scare me. If you convince me that I keep seeing something out of the corner of my eye and hear the faint rustle of movement behind me, in spite of my not being able to find anything there, and suggest the last owner of the house disappeared under mysterious circumstances, then it is far more likely that you'll have set me up to scare myself. (This is hard, which is why so many horror films use gore and jump-scares to such an extent.) So, if you can take that sort of logic and apply it to civilization-ending-things, then you're in business. The obvious flipside being that if you are too subtle, it will go right over some of your audience's heads, but that's a different problem. Unfortunately, in my opinion you haven't struck that balance.

Bottom line, I think the concept has lots of potential, but I recommend either pursuing a different direction from hiding-from-civilization-enders completely, or at the very least aiming to make that part more implicit than explicit (think "Fridge Horror").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another no-vote, also reluctant. The basic concept of a big-rock-that-speaks is an intriguing start, rife with possibilities, but it's a letdown that it just turns out to be some guy hiding from a bog-standard All-Destroyer-Who-Must-Not-Be-Named (literally, I slumped and groaned aloud on reaching "TO HIDE FROM THE DARK ONES").

That's the big issue, and then there's a passel of little nitpicks, minor in themselves, which together pile into a weight of disbelief too heavy to sustain:

  • A big stone pillar that undulates and sometimes spikes 250 meters high has escaped notice by the mundane world throughout all of human history?
  • A possibly Paleolithic individual, whose ancient precursor-Khoisan language almost certainly lacked a written form, communicates by transliterating its language in a "simple substitution" using binary code? A substitution of what — is it producing hexadecimal code for a Latin-alphabet phonetic spelling, or… what? It would be easier to believe if it just produced visible letters on its top surface.
  • This message in binary is "similar" to an unknown script? In what way?
  • Given the message "SENDWAVES", was the researchers' first thought to use gamma rays? Did they try radio first, or even just a big set of speakers?
  • Plan A is "a big tarp" and Plan B is "a 300m-high holographic projection"? What is a camouflage projection? Can it only be seen from directly above? What are their plans to hide this object from seismologists around the world?
  • What is noteworthy about the numbers mentioned in footnotes 2 and 3?

Und so weiter. Again, I really do like the core concept here, and I was right there with you even despite some of the peculiar bits, up until the collapsible section where it went all Shyamalan. Find some other identity and origin for the subterranean mass of post-perovskite and I think you can reform it into a winner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Speaking with my artist hat on, sometimes the best thing to do when you're too in love with an idea is to set it off to the side and come back to it later. A little dispassionate objectivity goes a long way in making tough calls like ditching an element you like or that you spent a lot of time working on in order to improve the piece as a whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this but I can't. Particularly:

  • Technobabble in the containment procedures is a big pet peeve of mine. I don't find it plausible that the Foundation has this useful ultra-tech, keeps it secret, and only uses it here (it's even "standard issue" technobabble here, if it is standard issue why isn't it used everywhere it would make sense?).
  • The final payoff of the communication with the entity is such a cliche letdown.

Reluctant downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tone is excellent throughout and the concept was good, but I too was a bit disappointed that it turned out to be a man in the rock, particularly the "they're coming! You can't run!" sort of dialogue in the interview. Not sure why he would go to all the bother, or even if he could. Surely just hiding in a big rock would do it?

No vote, although I do see you've put a great deal of effort into this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I genuinely thank you for your input. I understand the dialogue is the weakest part of the article, and it has taken a considerable amount of effort and revisions to get it to where it is now (even though it is still unsatisfactory). For example, look at the last parts of Revision 12 on my sandbox page: http://scpsandbox2.wikidot.com/ooblex

In the final parts of that revision, I wrote up what I believed to be the best piece of work I could think up. I was very proud of it. But in the end I had to make the difficult decision to leave that part out, due to numerous issues as well as Hornby not appreciating that it revealed extra information related to 1050. And that's not even the end of it. The draft itself is almost a full month in the making.

In summary, I did all I could. I know it could be improved, but I am too emotionally torn, unmotivated, and exhausted to do much to help it.

However, I really do appreciate your comments. They were helpful in seeing my article from different perspectives. I hope you reconsider your downvote, but if you don't, no problem. I understand.

e: Now I did all I could. I finished the new log and changed a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've given this a tentative downvote. I liked it a lot, without being completely hooked, through the end of the description - the science felt sufficiently real, it is an interesting object and it sounds rather pretty. I didn't love the 300m high cloak of invisibility, or the idea that we could detect 1cm movements through 3000km of rock, but I was prepared to roll with it.

But from the third paragraph of the dropdown onwards, I liked it less and less. I was uncomfortable with an ancient human deciding to use binary to communicate. I struggle to imagine a paleolithic culture that had quite such a wide vocabulary. I don't think that the crosslink to 1050 added anything (although fair play in getting permission and input). I don't know why we're communicating in gamma waves, rather than simply pushing back on the obsidian to form shapes at the far end. And I was quite disappointed at (a) the idea that this was as simple as a human intelligence in rock form; (b) the trope of "hide from something worse that I can't explain but it's horrible I promise"; and (c) cutting off the dialogue with "lonely and stupid and horrible" - it all felt quite arbitrary.

Sorry, that all sounds very complainy, but I feel like the ending spoiled the overall effect. I think I would upvote if this anomaly had a purpose other than a depressed human hiding from bogeymen.

EDIT: Okay, I should have reminded myself properly about 1050 - they're not just any bogeymen. The dialogue still doesn't quite work for me, but I'll reconsider my downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have been told that before. I tried to make the reader ask questions that he or she would need to read further in order to find the answers. I tried my best to execute it well.

e: I have removed that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the record, the only nitpick I really have is that I really don't like the use of cancellations in digital documents (it makes little sense in practice to strike out online data, when they can be outright deleted or archived).

That aside, I am mildly interested in the prospect of a potential Pattern Screamers skip and something about the early days of humanity.

item.title: Special Thanks
item.content:encoded:
Special thanks to Hornby, Wogglebug, GhostActual, and Michael Atreus. You guys are great.

Hornby, who is the author of SCP-1050, gave me permission to reference his article (no longer part of the article), helped me in a major way, and oversaw many of the revisions.

I drew inspiration from SCP-1050, SCP-163, and other articles.

PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING BEFORE VOTING OR COMMENTING:

"I know it is cliche, but only if you actually believe what -1 "tells" you. It could have no idea what it is talking about. It could be confused. It could be desperate for attention after who-knows-how-long alone and cannot maintain its composure for long until it ends up sulking and moping (I particularly like this one). It could be lying. The Foundation might be misinterpreting the patterns completely; perhaps -1 is not sentient at all, and the patterns on 2929 are simply random (although extremely improbable) products of a probabilistic universe. The reader and the Foundation simply do not know."
-ooblex, 2015

e: Changes to the log are in progress, but will not be complete for anywhere from a couple to 10 days due to reasons outside of my control.

ee: Finished! Please disregard any of my comments that no longer apply due to the edit.

eee: Thanks Decibelles for fixing a spelling error!
